ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general samosa entry (FOODON_03302051); no specific keema or lamb-filled variant exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers samosa as a category; no lamb-keema-specific facet code available. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for keema samosa or samosa as a food product at this level of specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium keema samosa weighing approximately 60 g, as typically served as a snack or starter in Indian restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Samosa, meat-filled, fried — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition); supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for lamb-filled fried pastry analogues
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) provides values for meat-filled samosa (fried). Values for a lamb-keema variant are adapted from the meat samosa entry, cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy data for minced lamb and fried wheat pastry to confirm macro plausibility. Per-100g values represent the composite fried product.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of samosa and similar fried wheat-pastry snacks — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for fried wheat-flour pastry products and meat-filled dim sum analogues
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for keema samosa exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 45 based on fried wheat-pastry analogues (spring rolls, meat-filled pastries) listed in the Sydney GI Database, which cluster in the GI 40–55 range. The protein and fat content of the lamb filling further attenuates glycemic response. GL per serving calculated as (45 × 14.7 g available carbohydrate per 60 g serving) / 100 = 6.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~380 mg/100 g) and deep-fried preparation
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit keema samosa intake to one piece occasionally, as the sodium content (~228 mg per 60 g serving) and saturated fat from deep frying can contribute to elevated blood pressure and vascular load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat flour pastry shell with moderate glycemic index (~45) and high fat content
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume keema samosa in strict moderation; although the GI is low (~45) due to fat and protein attenuation, the refined-flour shell and deep-frying contribute a significant caloric and carbohydrate load that can impair postprandial glucose control if portion size is not controlled.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~5.5 g/100 g), trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g), and cholesterol from deep frying and lamb mince
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit keema samosa due to its elevated saturated fat, trans fat, and cholesterol content from deep frying in oil and the use of fatty lamb mince, all of which are associated with adverse LDL cholesterol and atherosclerotic risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Keema samosa is a deep-fried Indian savoury pastry made from a thin, crisp shell of refined wheat flour (maida) encasing a spiced minced lamb (keema) filling. The filling is typically prepared by sautéing minced lamb with onion, garlic, ginger, green chilli, cumin, coriander, and garam masala until dry and aromatic, then sealed inside triangular pastry pockets and deep-fried in oil until golden and crisp. The result is a pastry with a shatteringly crisp exterior and a dense, warmly spiced, savoury meat interior. Keema samosa is a popular snack and starter across the Indian subcontinent and is widely available in Indian restaurants in Singapore, often served with mint chutney or tamarind sauce. Nutritionally, it is energy-dense (~280 kcal/100 g) with moderate protein (~10.5 g/100 g) from the lamb, significant fat (~16 g/100 g) from deep frying and the meat, and a moderate carbohydrate load (~24.5 g/100 g) from the pastry shell. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low), attenuated by fat and protein in the filling. Clinically, the dish is of moderate concern for hypertension due to sodium content, moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to refined carbohydrate and caloric density, and high concern for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat, trans fat, and cholesterol from deep frying and lamb fat. It is suitable for halal diets and contains gluten as a primary allergen.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a keema samosa?
What is the GI of samosa?
What is the difference between keema samosa and vegetable samosa?
What are the ingredients in keema samosa?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN meat samosa data (2017) cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy lamb and fried pastry entries. No exact keema samosa entry exists in a primary approved source; values represent a well-grounded adaptation. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database fried wheat-pastry analogues. Confidence is medium rather than high because no single approved source provides an exact keema samosa entry.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. Trans fat value of 0.2 g/100 g is an estimate based on typical deep-frying conditions in South Asian cooking; the ICMR-NIN source does not explicitly report trans fat for this item. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct measurement exists for keema samosa in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per serving = (45 × 14.7) / 100 = 6.6, where 14.7 g is the available carbohydrate in a 60 g serving (24.5 × 0.60). Halal status assumed based on lamb filling and typical preparation at Indian halal restaurants in Singapore; individual restaurant certification should be verified. The food is served by one restaurant on field survey data (Indian House/Dumpukht Grills & Curry, Boat Quay) at SGD 6.68.
